summary: Gruber pushes back on Alexis Madrigal's characterization of 11.9 million iPad sales as disappointing, arguing that fixating on analyst estimates of 13 million misses the bigger picture. He uses Tim Cook's historical comparisons to show the iPad reached 67 million units in two years — faster than the Mac, iPod, or iPhone. Gruber notes the iPad was still supply-constrained, meaning Apple sold every unit it could manufacture. He compares iPad sales at the two-year mark to iPhone sales at the same stage, showing the iPad is on an even steeper trajectory. He concludes that the evidence suggests the iPad is not merely an iPhone-level success but potentially a greater one. A footnote on Apple's profit growth further underscores how dramatically the company's scale had changed in just a few years.
key_points: Analyst consensus misses of 1 million units represent the difference between great and really great, not a meaningful failure
At the two-year mark, iPad quarterly sales of 11.9 million dwarfed the iPhone's 5.2 million at the same stage
The new iPad was supply-constrained for its entire availability period, meaning demand exceeded Apple's manufacturing capacity
Extrapolating from one's own personal usage patterns to broad market predictions is a common analytical error
Apple's quarterly profit growth from $1.67B record in 2009 to $11.6B just ten quarters later illustrates the company's explosive scaling
money_quotes: It's a severe mistake to get this caught up in analyst predictions. You will miss the forest for the tree-growth projections.
The difference between Apple having sold 12 million iPads versus 13 million iPads is, at worst, the difference between great and really great.
Which means Apple sold new iPads as fast as they could make them.
If you believe this 11.9 million unit sale number deserves to be called 'disappointing', you should be disappointed only by Apple's inability to manufacture them faster.
Other than that, there simply is no bad news for Apple in these results.
The evidence to date suggests not that the iPad is another iPhone-esque success for Apple, but that the iPad is an even greater success than the iPhone.
A sum of money that three years ago was a record quarterly profit is now just the blip between the historically strong holiday quarter and the historically weak January-March quarter.
key_insight: Comparing iPad sales to arbitrary analyst estimates rather than to historical product trajectories obscures the fact that the iPad was growing faster than any product in Apple's history.
